museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: The year closes with Quake II on PC, Gran Turismo launching in Japan, Grand Theft Auto on UK shelves, and Christmas lists split between PlayStation, N64, and the family computer.
museum.curatorNote: December is unusually dense but still region-sensitive. Quake II and Gran Turismo have strong late-December anchors; UK retail context is kept broad unless a source supports precision.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: The year ends by turning three futures into shelves: online shooters, driving simulation, and open-city crime.

items.title: Quake II releases on PC
items.body: id Software's shooter arrived in December, bringing the year's PC hardware talk into a playable object of coloured lighting, network play, and upgrade pressure.
items.certainty: verified month; exact regional days vary
items.sourceNote: Quake II references list December 1997, commonly December 9 in North America and December 12 in Europe.

items.title: Gran Turismo launches in Japan
items.body: Polyphony Digital's driving game arrived in Japan just before the year ended, pointing PlayStation toward a more simulation-minded future.
items.sourceNote: Official Gran Turismo pages list Japan as December 23, 1997.

items.title: Grand Theft Auto reaches the UK PC conversation
items.body: DMA Design's original GTA was a British-made provocation: top-down, rude, urban, and already generating the sense that games could be discussed as public trouble.
items.sourceNote: References list European MS-DOS/Windows release in late November 1997 and PlayStation in December 1997.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: December's release shelf is a clash of machines: PC acceleration, Japanese car obsession, and console Christmas pressure.

items.title: Quake II
items.body: A PC shooter that made graphics cards, network play, and industrial sci-fi feel like one package.
items.sourceNote: Exact regional day differs by source; kept as December 1997.

items.title: Gran Turismo
items.body: Japanese release of a driving game that would become a global PlayStation identity piece in 1998.
items.sourceNote: Official Gran Turismo page lists December 23, 1997.

items.title: Grand Theft Auto
items.body: A UK-developed crime sandbox whose exact platform timing varies by source and region, but whose late-1997 presence belongs in the Christmas exhibit.
items.certainty: verified late-1997 window; platform dates vary
items.sourceNote: Wikipedia and Take-Two materials place PC in November 1997 and PlayStation in December 1997.

items.title: Tomb Raider II
items.body: Still the shop-window sequel of the season in the UK, carrying Eidos' Christmas campaign into the final month.
items.sourceNote: November release context; included for Christmas retail momentum.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: December made hardware decisions feel like family economics.

items.title: PC graphics as Christmas pressure
items.body: Quake II made the family PC feel measurable: did it run, did it run well, and did it need a card?

items.title: Console choice under the tree
items.body: PlayStation offered breadth and cheaper CDs; N64 offered Mario, Kart, Bond, and first-party certainty.
items.sourceNote: Forbes and late-1997 retail context support PlayStation/N64 comparison.

items.title: Memory cards and save permanence
items.body: Long PlayStation games made saves feel fragile and valuable. A memory card could be the most important small object in the house.
items.sourceNote: Final Fantasy VII/Tomb Raider II seasonal context.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: December issues felt like buying guides disguised as prophecy.

items.title: Christmas buyer's guides
items.headline: PlayStation, N64 and PC CD-ROM under pressure
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed multiformat magazine-style cover image standing in for December 1997.
items.curatorNote: The magazine became a family negotiation aid: what is worth buying, what needs extra hardware, and what will last past Boxing Day.
items.body: The magazine became a family negotiation aid: what is worth buying, what needs extra hardware, and what will last past Boxing Day.

items.title: PC shooter coverage
items.headline: Quake II and the graphics card question
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed PC magazine-style cover image standing in for December 1997.
items.curatorNote: Quake II coverage made screenshots feel like hardware evidence.
items.body: Quake II coverage made screenshots feel like hardware evidence.
items.sourceNote: Based on December Quake II release context.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: December's PC future was connected, but still not effortless.

items.title: Quake II made servers feel like destinations
items.body: Deathmatch, patches, and performance talk moved through the same modem line that someone else might need for a phone call.

items.title: Christmas guides travelled by print and text
items.body: Walkthroughs and hints could be found online, but many players still trusted a magazine supplement or a printed FAQ.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: December 1997 as gift list, upgrade list, and wish list.

items.title: The year became a shelf
items.body: Final Fantasy VII, GoldenEye, Tomb Raider II, GTA, Quake II, and Gran Turismo did not feel like separate events anymore. They felt like a wall of possible futures.

items.title: Choosing a platform felt personal
items.body: PlayStation meant breadth, N64 meant rooms full of people, and PC meant the thrill and dread of system requirements.

sources.label: Grand Theft Auto release context
sources.note: Used for late-1997 PC/PlayStation regional context.

sources.label: Take-Two historical GTA listing
sources.note: Used for Grand Theft Auto PC November 1997 context.
